summary: I was born in a village called Mon-ya, Laos, in 1946. Laos was my home before the Vietnam War disrupted everything. My family and I had to flee Laos during the war, crossing the Mekong River to escape the conflict. Even after the war, two of my brothers stayed in Laos while I moved on with my family.

The Vietnam War was a turning point in my life and my family's. It started after my parents had passed away, and during that time, my husband, Pa Ying Vue, was a soldier fighting alongside the CIA and General Vang Pao. The war forced us to flee Laos and eventually cross into Thailand to seek refuge. It was a difficult and dangerous period that shaped much of my journey.

Pa Ying Vue was my husband and a soldier during the Vietnam War. We were married for about a year before having our first child, and together we had five children. He led a troop of 12 men to assist the CIA and General Vang Pao in fighting against the Lao troops. Sadly, he was killed in action while I was living in the jungles of Thailand with our family. We held a traditional funeral for him there before I moved my family to a refugee camp.

Before fleeing Laos, my family and I traveled to the village of Plum Pu Na Thon. It was there that we prepared to cross the Mekong River under the cover of night. I gathered bamboo, plastic bags, and rubber tubes to help us cross safely without being caught or drowning. This village was a crucial stop on our journey to escape the war.

The Mekong River was the barrier between my family’s life in Laos and our escape to safety in Thailand. We crossed it at about 11 o'clock at night, using bamboo, plastic bags, and rubber tubes to stay afloat and avoid detection. Crossing the river was terrifying but necessary to flee the war and find refuge. It marked the beginning of our long journey through the jungles of Thailand.

During the Vietnam War, my husband worked closely with the Central Intelligence Agency. He led a troop of men to assist the CIA and General Vang Pao in fighting against the Lao troops. The CIA’s involvement was part of the secret war in Laos, and it was because of my husband’s service with them that we later received help immigrating to the United States. Their role was significant in the conflict and in our family’s story.

General Vang Pao was a key figure during the Vietnam War, and my husband fought alongside him. He led a troop that helped the CIA and General Vang Pao fight against the Lao troops. Vang Pao’s leadership was important in the conflict that forced my family to flee Laos. His name came up often during the interview process for immigration because of my husband’s connection to him.

After crossing the Mekong River at night, my family and I entered Thailand. We walked through the jungles there for five days without food or shelter before finding a safe place to rest. We lived in the jungles of Thailand for about three years while hiding from danger. Eventually, the Thai government placed us in the Nom Kai refugee camp, where we stayed until we were approved to immigrate to the United States.

The Nom Kai refugee camp in Thailand was where my family and I lived after spending years in the jungle. We were placed there by the Thai government around 1977. Life in the camp was difficult, with barbed wire fences, long food lines, and crowded barracks. We stayed there for about a year and a half while waiting for approval to immigrate to the United States. The camp was a place of hardship but also hope for a better future.

In 1979, my family and I were approved to immigrate to the United States. We first lived in Des Moines, Iowa, where the government helped us with basic needs like rent and groceries. After a couple of months, we moved to Tulsa, Oklahoma, to be near relatives, and later to La Crosse, Wisconsin, where we stayed for over a decade. Now I live in Hudson, Wisconsin, with my youngest son and his family. Life in America has been a blessing compared to the hardships we faced in Laos and Thailand.

Mr. Thao was a Hmong man who sponsored my family’s immigration to the United States. He helped us because he didn’t want my children to suffer or starve in Thailand after my husband was killed. Without his support, it would have been much harder for us to start a new life in America. I am very grateful for his kindness during such a difficult time.

When we first arrived in the United States in 1979, we lived in Des Moines, Iowa for about two months. The government provided us with funds to cover rent, food, and other basic needs during that time. It was our first home in America and a place where we began adjusting to a new life after years of hardship. Though brief, our stay there was important as a starting point for our resettlement.

After living in Iowa, my family and I moved to Tulsa, Oklahoma, where we stayed for about three to four years. We moved there because we had relatives in the area, which made settling in easier. Tulsa was where my children grew up a bit more and where we started building a community. It was an important chapter in our new life in America.

We later moved to La Crosse, Wisconsin, where we lived for most of our lives, around 10 to 12 years. It was during this time that my daughters got married while my sons remained bachelors. La Crosse became a stable home for us after many years of moving and hardship. It holds many memories of our family’s growth and resilience in America.

Now I live in Hudson, Wisconsin with my youngest son, Xai Vue. He is married with three children, and I help watch my grandchildren. Hudson is where I have found peace and happiness later in life. It’s a place where my family continues to thrive and where I feel grateful for the journey that brought us here.
